text.tspan: Design and Implementation of a Multi-GPU
Romain PEREIRA, Swann PERARNAU, Mathematics and Computer Science Division, Argonne National Laboratory
Pierre-Etienne POLLET, Thierry GAUTIER, Inria, LIP, Lyon, France

tspan.tspan: Multi-GPU systems dominate the HPC and AI computing facilities due to their
energy efficiency
. The variery of vendors and hardware complexity creates
a challenge in conceiving performing and portable applications.
Macro data-flow task-based systems are a promising solution to meet this
challenge, delegating the responsibility from user-code to architecture-aware
compilers and runtime systems.
Workloads are split into dependent tasks and distributed on available
processors, implicitly overlapping computation with transfers following tasks
accesses data-flow
This poster introduces a fork of Kaapi
for macro-dataflow and task-based
programming on multi-gpu architectures with support for AMD/Intel/Nvidia
GPUs and networks (nvlink / xe-link).
We present the runtime system's internal abstractions towards achieving
performance portability, an extension for 2-dimensional task accesses, and
illustrate it with XKBlas: a multi-GPU BLAS library.

text.tspan: Towards a single UNIX process on multi-GPU compute blades
An Aurora compute-blade - current recommendations are 1x MPI per tile
Execution model based on a 6-state task system

tspan.tspan: SM Clock for n=77,824
FP64 GEMM performances on 4x H100-SXM GPU
(700W) and an Intel(R) Xeon(R) Platinum 8468 CPU.

text.tspan: Adds run-time management overhead against state-of-the-art [2,7]
1) Conflicts detection over 2 dimensional memory regions
2) Coherency between memories minimizing data transfers

text.tspan: Performance portability across AMD/Nvidia multi-gpu systems.
Extending to n-dimensional accesses for stencil scientific simulation.
OpenMP extensions for implicit data-flow and multi-GPU execution.
